channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - kotaku.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for kotaku.com

item.title: Grand Theft Auto Fans Are Losing It Today Over A Rumored GTA 6 Pre-Order Announcement That Seems Unlikelier By The Minute
item.content:encoded:
Last week, GTA fans were whipping themselves into a hype frenzy over multiple plausible reports of Best Buy emails advertising that Grand Theft Auto VI pre-orders would be going live today. But it’s now 10am ET, there are no pre-orders to be found, and those same fans are starting to spiral as it starts to look more and more likely that the origins of this rumor were just a Best Buy screw-up after all.

While the GTA community has collectively lost its mind before over totally ridiculous things that were supposedly hinting at GTA 6 updates—things like tea cafe foot traffic and the positions of the actual real-life planets—this pre-order rumor actually seemed somewhat plausible. The source was a number of individuals all separately sharing emails from Best Buy offering a 5-percent discount to individuals pre-ordering physical copies of GTA 6 from the store between May 18 and May 21. Several of those claiming to have received the emails went to great lengths to show that the emails were legit, including one person livestreaming themselves actively opening the email on their phone. There’s a whole Reddit thread of people showing proof.

But with no pre-orders live yet today, distress is starting to creep in again. This is being exacerbated by a post on GTAForums from a user claiming that “internal company emails suggest that Best Buy has got it wrong and no pre-orders will be launching today.” While that wouldn’t mean much coming from a random poster, forum mods have stated they verified this person actually is in a position to be receiving internal Best Buy communications and is probably telling the truth.

The collective disappointment is strong enough that fans are poring over any crumb they can find suggesting there might be pre-orders today, or at least soon. One person pointed out that the Xbox GTA 6 page was apparently updated just last night, which could mean…anything, I guess. Also from last night, someone thinks they’ve found what will be the GTA 6 official page on Walmart’s website, though to what end, no one really knows.

Other people are coping in the only way they know how: memes.

The thing is, none of these people are wrong to expect something soon-ish. Take-Two has repeatedly said that GTA 6 marketing would spin up this summer, and we’re over halfway through May. Sony has already been sending marketing emails and notifications encouraging people to buy a PS5 for GTA 6. We’re also over 365 days now from the last time we saw anything official of GTA 6, and tomorrow will mark exactly six months until the currently planned release date of November 19. And Take-Two’s earnings call is on Thursday, which means another opportunity for overeager fans to get a crumb of a statement about this game.

It seems like something actual and substantial related to GTA 6 could pop up any day now, though even when it does, we’ve still got a long six months ahead of us before all this pent-up energy is finally let loose into Leonida. Stay cool, folks.



https://kotaku.com/gta-6-fans-rumored-preorder-announcement-today-2000697037
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Subnautica 2 Has A Violence Problem, And It’s Not Coming From The Players
item.content:encoded:
One aspect of Subnautica 2 that has strongly stood out to me as different from the original game is just how dangerously violent so many of the alien fish are. The first Subnautica was not without its aggressive sealife, especially the colossal kind, but you also had at least a weeny little knife to wave around in self-defense. In Subnautica 2 you’ve got flares, and the more I play, the more I’m finding myself frustrated by the constant annoyance of angry fishies trying to bash into me. It seems I’m not alone, and as IGN points out, there’s a growing number of players asking for the ability to fight back.

It was never the point of the original Subnautica to kill anything. The game was overtly pacifist in its design, and while you could get a knife very early on, it was primarily for cutting plant-life. However, it did also double up as a means of some defense against particularly aggressive animals that didn’t want to share their patch of ocean with you. Or, if you were a bad person, you could repeatedly swipe at the sealife and eventually kill it. Not so in the mostly impressive Subnautica 2, where the only means of evading such attacks are swimming away, or lobbing flares that very temporarily distract the irritating aquatics. You can also sort-of give them a blast from you Sonic Resonator, but it has very minimal effect. And while I’m fully in support of a Subnautica in which I neither can nor even want to kill anything, right now at this point of its Early Access release, the balance is definitely off.

IGN quotes Subnautica environmental artist uly as saying that the ability to kill fish remains a lively discussion within the development studio. “Trust me it is a HOT topic,” they quote the artist as saying. And while this particular developer is on the side of letting players kill the smaller fish (which might sound odd until you recognize that you can catch them, cook them, and eat them, which is also rather killy), they’re against being able to fight the shark-sized predators. However, “When you bump into a predator you shouldn’t be going ‘uuuuuuugh what a pain,’ you should be going, ‘Ah man…what tools do I have up my sleeve to deal with this guy?'”

I very strongly agree. Because right now as I play, “uuuuuugh” is a far too common refrain. The issue is, creatures like the Bullethead and Marrowbreach are obsessive pests with vastly too much power. So you’re trying to complete a required task that involves staying in the same spot, and these buggers are incessantly ramming into you, biting you, electrocuting, poisoning and stinging you, and the only thing you can do is wave a flare. This is especially the case when it comes to later tasks involving clearing viral bloom from infected angel combs, where you are outrageously vulnerable the entire time with no recourse. So instead of feeling like you’re bravely surviving, instead you just feel helpless and annoyed, and that can’t be the angle developer Unknown Worlds is aiming for. Instead of seeing larger, aggressive fish as an interesting challenge, I see them as something that’s just actively spoiling the game for me.

The same is true, in fact, with the far larger sea monsters. The Collector Leviathan whom you encounter early on is at first a very exciting creature to meet, terrifyingly huge and extraordinarily dangerous, making for a thrilling Subnautica moment. But then as you’re required by the game’s overly pushy plot to repeatedly go through its territory, it quickly becomes a tiresome burden. I read one site advising players that they could get past it undisturbed by bunny-hopping their Tadpole crafts above the surface of the sea, which is just about the most ridiculous way to have to play. I’ve taken to diving super-deep, because at least there’s a chance of seeing something interesting, and then just hoping that the creature, which is significantly faster than my souped-up craft, doesn’t grab me that time. I should be looking forward to these encounters! There should be a technique I’ve learned for putting it off—flashing my headlights at it such that it gets upset, or a tool I can use to make a sonic explosion—which then makes passing it more academic after the first couple of times.

There will be those out there who just want a sea-gun, and I hope and assume that they will remain disappointed. Subnautica 2 is not about fighting the environment, but rather embracing it in order to survive. I have no desire to kill these animals (other than for my tea), but I also don’t want my experience playing the game to be so frequently sullied by the tedium they currently introduce. What if they’re upset by bright lights, and I can craft an extra-powerful flare that’ll keep them all at a distance while I get on with a task? Or perhaps some sort of sonic weapon that upsets them enough to make them want to swim off? Let it come at a cost, let it be tough to craft, or require frequent charging. This isn’t about cheesing the game. It’s about having an in-game method to enjoy the experience an awful lot more than the game is currently offering.



https://kotaku.com/subnautica-2-how-to-kill-fish-defense-attacks-violence-2000697003
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Credits Show Batman: Arkham Studio Helped Out On Lego Batman
item.content:encoded:
All of the trailers, previews, and interviews have made Lego Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight feel like a sort of successor to the Batman: Arkham games. Well, according to the game’s credits, that influence goes beyond just taking inspiration from the open-world crime-fighting blockbusters. Arkham maker Rocksteady Studios appears to have directly helped out on the game.

According to VGC, Rocksteady is listed as a co-developer on Lego Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight in the game’s credits. Roughly 24 developers, including a producer and multiple designers, are listed. Warner Bros. Games Montreal, which made Batman: Arkham Origins and Gotham Knights, is also listed as having provided development support.

None of that is shocking considering Lego Batman maker TT Games is part of Warner Bros., and co-development between studios under a shared publisher umbrella has become increasingly common as the budgets and scopes for blockbuster releases swell. Still, it reinforces that Lego Batman isn’t just mimicking the blueprint for a sandbox comic-book adaptation laid out by Rocksteady.

It’s not just that Lego Batman has an explorable Gotham City that lets you glide off skyscrapers and hookshot over buildings. The game’s prompt-based combat is also very reminiscent of that in the Arkham series. And we aren’t likely to get another one of those at least for a few more years. While Rocksteady pivoted back to the franchise after Suicide Squad: Kill the Justice League flopped, it feels like new blockbusters these days take five years or longer to ship.



https://kotaku.com/credits-show-batman-arkham-studio-helped-out-on-lego-batman-2000697012
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: GTA 6 Was Originally Supposed To Be Out A Year Ago Before Facing 18 Month Delay
item.content:encoded:
In another world, Grand Theft Auto 6 has already been out for nearly a year. Take-Two CEO Strauss Zelnick referenced that alternate timeline in a recent interview looking back at his career, in which he confirmed that Rockstar Games’ latest sequel was at one point supposed to come out 18 months before its current release date.

“I think we’re about 18 months behind the original date,” he told podcaster David Senra in a video published over the weekend. This would put GTA 6‘s original targeted launch at May 2025 alongside Doom: The Dark Ages and Elden Ring: Nightreign. The executive was asked about the pressure that comes with making those calls to delay a massive moneymaker, but he deflected instead to talking about GTA Online‘s continued popularity and how the power of social gaming brings people together (Zelnick’s mother plays Bridge online with people she’s never met).

But GTA 6 didn’t come out in May 2025. Instead, it was slated for fall of that year, then delayed to May 2026, and finally delayed to November 19, 2026. The first trailer for the game was released back in December 2023, and we didn’t get a new trailer until May 2025 which, based on Zelnick’s latest comments, would have been when the game was originally supposed to be out.

While it’s anyone’s guess whether that means we’ll get a third GTA 6 trailer this month ahead of Take-Two’s earnings call, that’s certainly every fan’s hope at the moment. Although Rockstar Games’ marketing for the sequel is expected to ramp up over the summer and into the fall, there’s also fear that GTA 6 could still get delayed a third time (or fourth, based on Zelnick’s math).

“You’re sitting on the most valuable media property in the world and everybody online is just like, ‘When’s GTA 6 coming out?’ How do you deal with the timelines? The answer to that question is just, ‘I don’t know,’ right?” Senra said on the podcast. But Zelnick held firm. “Well, no. November 19. I do know.” Fans are hoping that confidence means things are as locked in as they’ll ever be for what might be the biggest video game launch ever.



https://kotaku.com/gta-6-was-originally-supposed-to-be-out-a-year-ago-before-facing-18-month-delay-2000697004
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
